sections.title: Analyzing Arguments
items: Premises — statements analyzed to form a conclusion
Conclusion — result after analyzing premises
Major term — appears in one premise; is the predicate of the conclusion
Minor term — appears in one premise; is the subject of the conclusion
Middle term — appears in both premises but not in the conclusion
Syllogism — form of reasoning using two premises to make a logical conclusion

sections.title: Types of Syllogism
items: Categorical Syllogism: If A is B, and B is C, therefore A is C. Example: Sparky is a dog. Dogs can bark. Therefore, Sparky can bark.
Disjunctive Syllogism: A is either B or C. A is not C. Therefore, A is B.
Conditional — Modus Ponens: If A is true, then B is true. A is true. Therefore, B is true.
Conditional — Modus Tollens: If A is true, then B is true. B is NOT true. Therefore, A is NOT true.
Conditional — Hypothetical Chain: If A is true, then B is true. If B is true, then C is true. Therefore, if A is true, then C is true.
INVALID: Affirming the consequent OR denying the antecedent.

sections.title: Venn Diagram
items: 'All As are Bs' → Circle A is entirely inside Circle B.
'Some As are Bs' → Circles A and B partially overlap.
Draw each statement's diagram, then combine to make inferences.
Example: All kids are energetic. Some humans are kids. → Some humans are energetic.

sections.title: Diagramming Characters
items: Used when a problem involves multiple variables with varying features (ages, heights, rankings).
Step 1: Make a line and assign a direction (e.g., oldest at top).
Step 2: Write the first character in the middle.
Step 3: Analyze each statement and place characters based on comparisons.
Step 4: Create your conclusion based on the diagram.

sections.title: Ancient/Alien Language Problems
items: Find words that appear at least twice across different sentences.
These repeated words help establish the sentence structure and meaning of each word.
Match the position of the word in the sentence with the position of its equivalent in the translation.
Use process of elimination for remaining words.

sections.title: Problem Solving Strategies
items: Working Backwards: Used when a series of events leads to a known final value. Reverse all operations.
Make an Organized List: List all possible values, then eliminate based on each description.
Making a Diagram: Useful for visualizing motion or distance problems.
